root_text.sn47.13:1.1: Ekaṁ samayaṁ bhagavā sāvatthiyaṁ viharati jetavane anāthapiṇḍikassa ārāme.
root_text.sn47.13:1.2: Tena kho pana samayena āyasmā sāriputto magadhesu viharati nālakagāmake ābādhiko dukkhito bāḷhagilāno.
root_text.sn47.13:1.3: Cundo ca samaṇuddeso āyasmato sāriputtassa upaṭṭhāko hoti.
root_text.sn47.13:2.1: Atha kho āyasmā sāriputto teneva ābādhena parinibbāyi.
root_text.sn47.13:2.2: Atha kho cundo samaṇuddeso āyasmato sāriputtassa pattacīvaramādāya yena sāvatthi jetavanaṁ anāthapiṇḍikassa ārāmo yenāyasmā ānando tenupasaṅkami; upasaṅkamitvā āyasmantaṁ ānandaṁ abhivādetvā ekamantaṁ nisīdi. Ekamantaṁ nisinno kho cundo samaṇuddeso āyasmantaṁ ānandaṁ etadavoca:
root_text.sn47.13:3.1: “Atthi kho idaṁ, āvuso cunda, kathāpābhataṁ bhagavantaṁ dassanāya.
root_text.sn47.13:3.2: Āyāmāvuso cunda, yena bhagavā tenupasaṅkamissāma; upasaṅkamitvā bhagavato etamatthaṁ ārocessāmā”ti.
root_text.sn47.13:3.3: “Evaṁ, bhante”ti kho cundo samaṇuddeso āyasmato ānandassa paccassosi.
root_text.sn47.13:4.1: Atha kho āyasmā ca ānando cundo ca samaṇuddeso yena bhagavā tenupasaṅkamiṁsu; upasaṅkamitvā bhagavantaṁ abhivādetvā ekamantaṁ nisīdiṁsu. Ekamantaṁ nisinno kho āyasmā ānando bhagavantaṁ etadavoca:
root_text.sn47.13:4.5: Api ca me, bhante, madhurakajāto viya kāyo, disāpi me na pakkhāyanti, dhammāpi maṁ nappaṭibhanti ‘āyasmā sāriputto parinibbuto’ti sutvā”.
root_text.sn47.13:5.1: “Kiṁ nu kho te, ānanda, sāriputto sīlakkhandhaṁ vā ādāya parinibbuto, samādhikkhandhaṁ vā ādāya parinibbuto, paññākkhandhaṁ vā ādāya parinibbuto, vimuttikkhandhaṁ vā ādāya parinibbuto, vimuttiñāṇadassanakkhandhaṁ vā ādāya parinibbuto”ti?
root_text.sn47.13:5.2: “Na ca kho me, bhante, āyasmā sāriputto sīlakkhandhaṁ vā ādāya parinibbuto, samādhikkhandhaṁ vā …pe…
root_text.sn47.13:5.6: Api ca me, bhante, āyasmā sāriputto ovādako ahosi otiṇṇo viññāpako sandassako samādapako samuttejako sampahaṁsako, akilāsu dhammadesanāya, anuggāhako sabrahmacārīnaṁ.
root_text.sn47.13:5.7: Taṁ mayaṁ āyasmato sāriputtassa dhammojaṁ dhammabhogaṁ dhammānuggahaṁ anussarāmā”ti.
root_text.sn47.13:6.3: Taṁ kutettha, ānanda, labbhā. Yaṁ taṁ jātaṁ bhūtaṁ saṅkhataṁ palokadhammaṁ, taṁ vata mā palujjīti—
root_text.sn47.13:6.5: Seyyathāpi, ānanda, mahato rukkhassa tiṭṭhato sāravato yo mahantataro khandho so palujjeyya;
root_text.sn47.13:6.6: evameva kho ānanda, mahato bhikkhusaṅghassa tiṭṭhato sāravato sāriputto parinibbuto.
root_text.sn47.13:6.7: Taṁ kutettha, ānanda, labbhā. Yaṁ taṁ jātaṁ bhūtaṁ saṅkhataṁ palokadhammaṁ, taṁ vata mā palujjī’ti—
root_text.sn47.13:6.9: Tasmātihānanda, attadīpā viharatha attasaraṇā anaññasaraṇā, dhammadīpā dhammasaraṇā anaññasaraṇā.
root_text.sn47.13:7.1: Kathañcānanda, bhikkhu attadīpo viharati attasaraṇo anaññasaraṇo, dhammadīpo dhammasaraṇo anaññasaraṇo?
root_text.sn47.13:7.2: Idhānanda, bhikkhu kāye kāyānupassī viharati ātāpī sampajāno satimā, vineyya loke abhijjhādomanassaṁ;
root_text.sn47.13:7.5: dhammesu dhammānupassī viharati ātāpī sampajāno satimā, vineyya loke abhijjhādomanassaṁ.
root_text.sn47.13:7.6: Evaṁ kho, ānanda, bhikkhu attadīpo viharati attasaraṇo anaññasaraṇo, dhammadīpo dhammasaraṇo anaññasaraṇo.
root_text.sn47.13:7.7: Ye hi keci, ānanda, etarahi vā mamaccaye vā attadīpā viharissanti attasaraṇā anaññasaraṇā, dhammadīpā dhammasaraṇā anaññasaraṇā; tamatagge mete, ānanda, bhikkhū bhavissanti ye keci sikkhākāmā”ti.

translation_text.sn47.13:1.1: At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery.
translation_text.sn47.13:1.2: At that time Venerable Sāriputta was staying in the Magadhan lands near the little village of Nālaka, and he was sick, suffering, gravely ill.
translation_text.sn47.13:1.3: And the novice Cunda was his carer.
translation_text.sn47.13:2.1: Then Venerable Sāriputta was fully extinguished from that sickness.
translation_text.sn47.13:2.2: Then Cunda took Sāriputta’s bowl and robes and set out for Sāvatthī. He went to see Venerable Ānanda at Jeta’s grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him:
translation_text.sn47.13:2.3: “Sir, Venerable Sāriputta has become fully quenched.
translation_text.sn47.13:2.4: This is his bowl and robe.”
translation_text.sn47.13:3.1: “Reverend Cunda, we should see the Buddha about this matter.
translation_text.sn47.13:3.2: Come, let’s go to the Buddha and inform him about this.”
translation_text.sn47.13:4.1: Then Ānanda and Cunda went to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him:
translation_text.sn47.13:4.3: Venerable Sāriputta has become fully quenched.
translation_text.sn47.13:4.4: This is his bowl and robe.
translation_text.sn47.13:4.5: Since I heard this, my body feels like it’s drugged. I’m disorientated, and the teachings don’t spring to mind.”
translation_text.sn47.13:5.1: “Well, Ānanda, when Sāriputta was fully quenched, did he take away your entire spectrum of ethical conduct, of immersion, of wisdom, of freedom, or of the knowledge and vision of freedom?”
translation_text.sn47.13:5.6: But Venerable Sāriputta was my adviser and counselor. He educated, encouraged, fired up, and inspired me. He never tired of teaching the Dhamma, and he supported his spiritual companions.
translation_text.sn47.13:5.7: I remember the nectar of the teaching, the riches of the teaching, the support of the teaching given by Venerable Sāriputta.”
translation_text.sn47.13:6.1: “Ānanda, did I not prepare for this when I explained that
translation_text.sn47.13:6.2: we must be parted and separated from all we hold dear and beloved?
translation_text.sn47.13:6.3: How could it possibly be so that what is born, created, conditioned, and liable to wear out should not wear out?
translation_text.sn47.13:6.5: Suppose there was a large tree standing with heartwood, and the largest branch broke off.
translation_text.sn47.13:6.6: In the same way, in the great Saṅgha that stands with heartwood, Sāriputta has become fully quenched.
translation_text.sn47.13:6.7: How could it possibly be so that what is born, created, conditioned, and liable to wear out should not wear out?
translation_text.sn47.13:6.9: So Ānanda, live as your own island, your own refuge, with no other refuge. Let the teaching be your island and your refuge, with no other refuge.
translation_text.sn47.13:7.1: And how does a mendicant do this?
translation_text.sn47.13:7.2: It’s when a mendicant meditates by observing an aspect of the body—keen, aware, and mindful, rid of covetousness and displeasure for the world.
translation_text.sn47.13:7.3: They meditate observing an aspect of feelings …
translation_text.sn47.13:7.5: principles—keen, aware, and mindful, rid of covetousness and displeasure for the world.
translation_text.sn47.13:7.6: That’s how a mendicant lives as their own island, their own refuge, with no other refuge. That’s how the teaching is their island and their refuge, with no other refuge.
translation_text.sn47.13:7.7: Whether now or after I have passed, any who shall live as their own island, their own refuge, with no other refuge; with the teaching as their island and their refuge, with no other refuge—those mendicants of mine who want to train shall be among the best of the best.”
